coredata.dc:title: Preparation of boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yueying
Hu, Xuetao
Li, Wenting
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Zhang, Wen
Zhang, Xinai
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent probe based on the static quenching and the inner filter effect between boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N-CDs) and Cr(VI) was developed for the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in real water samples. B, N-CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method with ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato) diboron as raw materials. Compared with undoped CDs, the fluorescence properties of the B, N-CDs were improved. The fluorescence quantum yield of the B, N-CDs was as high as 59.01%. After optimization of the experimental parameters, the B, N-CDs could be used as a fluorescence probe to detect Cr(VI). Strong linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9986) was established in the Cr(VI) concentration range 0.3–500 μM, and a detection limit of 0.24 μM was achieved. Moreover, the B, N-CDs successfully detected Cr(VI) in real water samples, indicating that they have broad application prospects in the sensitive detection of Cr(VI).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
High fluorescence quantum yield
Static quenching
Inner filter effect
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of B, N-CDs
Optical properties of the B, N-CDs
Sensitivity and selectivity of B, N-CDs for Cr(VI) detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of B, N-CDs. The illustration is the corresponding photographs of B, N-CDs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence emission wavelength of B, N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(A) TEM images and (B) particles size distribution of the synthesized B, N-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of B, N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D), and B1s (E). (F) FT-IR spectra of B, N-CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) with excitation and emission spectra of B, N-CDs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of different materials. (C) Fluorescence lifetime of B, N-CDs in the presence or absence of Cr(VI).

(A) Fluorescence emission of B, N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI). (B) The relation curve between the fluorescence intensity ratio of B, N-CDs and Cr(VI) concentration, in which F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs without and with Cr(VI), respectively. The inset of (B) shows the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and Cr(VI) concentration.
Fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions towards B, N-CDs.

(A) The relationship between (F
0 − F)/F
0 and the concentration of Cr(VI) of Yudai River water sample (with pretreatment). (B) The relationship between (F
0 − F)/F
0 and the concentration of Cr(VI) of the tap water samples (with pretreatment).
Schematic illustration of fluorescent B, N-CDs for detection of Cr(VI) ions.
Comparison with the results for the determination of Cr(VI) by other methods.

head.ce:title: Preparation of boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid detection of Cr(VI)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent probe based on the static quenching and the inner filter effect between boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N-CDs) and Cr(VI) was developed for the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in real water samples. B, N-CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method with ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato) diboron as raw materials. Compared with undoped CDs, the fluorescence properties of the B, N-CDs were improved. The fluorescence quantum yield of the B, N-CDs was as high as 59.01%. After optimization of the experimental parameters, the B, N-CDs could be used as a fluorescence probe to detect Cr(VI). Strong linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9986) was established in the Cr(VI) concentration range 0.3–500 μM, and a detection limit of 0.24 μM was achieved. Moreover, the B, N-CDs successfully detected Cr(VI) in real water samples, indicating that they have broad application prospects in the sensitive detection of Cr(VI).

The fluorescence of B, N-CD prepared by hydrothermal synthesis of ammonium citrate and bis(pinacolato) diboron can be quenched in a concentration-dependent manner by Cr(VI) based on the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism.
Unlabelled Image

ce:keyword.ce:text: Boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the rapid development of modern industry, heavy metal pollution of soil and crops is becoming increasingly severe. Heavy metals can enter the body through skin contact, food consumption, breathing, and other ways and threaten the health of humans and animals [1]. The heavy metal chromium is an essential microelement for the human body, and the daily chromium requirement for adults is estimated to be 0.5–2 μg of absorbable chromium(III) [2]. Appropriate intake of trivalent chromium [Cr(III)] can promote human lipid metabolism and reduce the content of triglyceride and cholesterol in the blood. However, hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] has high toxicity, approximately 500–1000 times than that of Cr(III) [3]. Cr(VI) is not biodegradable and accumulates excessively in vivo through bioaccumulation, leading to acute injury [4]. Cr(VI) can interact with proteins and enzymes in the body, making them inactive and even harmful. The provisional guideline value proposed by the World Health Organization for total human chromium intake is 0.05 mg/L [2]. Therefore, a method of rapid and quantitative Cr(VI) detection is urgently needed.

Currently, numerous analytical techniques are available for detecting Cr(VI), including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [6], spectrophotometry, and atomic absorption spectrometry [7]. ICP-MS and ICP-OES have high detection sensitivity and yield accurate results, but the detection process and operation are complex and the instruments and equipment are expensive; additionally, they require laboratory conditions, making rapid and daily on-site detection difficult [8]. Diphenylcarbazide spectrophotometry is the most traditional method for the determination of hexavalent chromium, but its detection limit is high and it is strongly affected by the environment in the detection process [9]. Fluorescence analysis is a burgeoning method for the determination of trace metal ions. It has favorable sensitivity, strong selectivity, convenient operation, and wide application prospects [10].

The detection of heavy metal ions through fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention in recent years because of its simplicity, speed, and strong ability to resist environmental interference. Ayaz Ahmed et al. [11] developed a fluorescence sensor based on cadmium sulfide quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI). Phan et al. [12] proposed a simple one-step synthesis method to prepare fluorescent silicon quantum dots with a quantum yield of 14.2%, and used it as the probe for fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples. However, the above fluorescent materials have biological toxicity, poor optical stability and solubility [13]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are known to be effective substitutes for traditional fluorescent materials because of their tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity and excellent anti-interference ability [14,15]. However, the emission intensity of CDs is insufficient. The element doping method has been widely used to adjust the fluorescence properties of CDs and is considered to be the most effective method of producing CDs with excellent fluorescence properties [15]. In most of these dopings, boron (B) or nitrogen (N) is the natural choice for three reasons. First, B and N are the nearest neighbors of the C in the periodic table. Second, the atomic radii of B and N are similar to C. Third, the substitution of C by B or N eventually changes the local chemical reactivity, which enhances the optical performance [16]. Therefore, the fluorescence performance of CDs was improved from the perspective of B, N diatomic co-doping.

In this study, we proposed a simple hydrothermal method synthesis of boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (B, N-CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield and their applications in quantitatively detection of Cr(VI). The fluorescence of the B, N-CDs is quenched by Cr(VI) in a concentration-dependent manner through the static quenching mechanism and the inner filter effect (IFE), enabling quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in water. In addition, we compared and analyzed the performance of the B, N-CDs with that of undoped CDs by applied the B, N-CDs to the detection of Cr(VI) in real environmental water samples to study their selectivity and sensitivity.

Ammonium citrate (AC), bis(pinacolato) diboron 97% (BPDB), and the standard of hexavalent chromium stock solution were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Ourchem, China). Quinine sulfate and metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were analytical pure grade, and the experimental water employed was ultrapure water.

Fluorescent spectra were obtained using the F98 fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang Technology, Shanghai, China). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using the UV-1601 spectrometer (Beifen-Ruili, Beijing, China). The morphology and structure of the B, N-CDs were characterized using the JEM-2000 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The size distribution of the B, N-CDs was estimated using Nano Measurer 1.2 software. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using the Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, MA, USA). Fluorescence lifetime was measured using the QuantaMaster™40 Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer (PTI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed using the ESCALAB 250XI instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The test results for actual water samples were verified using Agilent 7800 ICP-MS (Agilent Technology, China).

On the basis of previous research, a simple hydrothermal method was used to synthesize B, N-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield [17]. First, dissolved 100 mg AC and 200 mg BPDB in 5 mL ultrapure water and mixed evenly, after which the solution was transferred to a para-polyphenol-lined high-pressure autoclave. The temperature was then steadily raised to 220 °C at the rate of 15 °C/min and maintained at that temperature for 10 h. The solution was subsequently cooled to room temperature naturally in a fume hood and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, which was filtered three times using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to remove some large particles. Then, the supernatant of B, N-CDs was freeze-dried for 12–36 h to obtain a light-yellow powder, which was stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate dihydrate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (fluorescence quantum yield (QY) = 54%, 350 nm, η = 1.33) was used as the standard. First, we prepared five samples each of quinine sulfate and B, N-CDs with an absorbance of 0.01–0.05 at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm; then, the fluorescence spectra at 360–600 nm of the prepared samples were obtained using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Subsequently, the integrated fluorescence intensity was calculated according to the fluorescence spectrum, and the integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against solution absorbance. Linear fitting was performed to determine the gradient of the curve, and the QY of B, N-CDs was calculated. The formula is as follows [18]:
(1)
Q =
Q R
Grad
Grad R
η 2
η R 2
where Q is the quantum yield, the subscript R refers to the reference standard of the known quantum yield, Grad is the gradient obtained from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus solution absorbance, and η is the solution's refractive index.

At room temperature (25 °C), 100 μL of B, N-CDs was mixed with 900 μL of buffer solution containing Cr(VI) at various concentrations. The buffer solution was used to adjust the pH of the mixture. After incubation for 8 min, a fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to detect the fluorescence intensity of the solution at the specific excitation wavelength. In the interference study, the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) was measured using a similar method. A schematic of the detection mechanism is displayed in Scheme 1 .

The real water samples with pretreatment from the Yudai River at Jiangsu University and tap water were selected to evaluate the effect of the B, N-CD method for detecting Cr(VI). The pretreatment process was as follows: first, centrifugation was performed at 8500 rpm for 15 min; second, the Yudai River water as filtered three times using a 0.22 μm microporous filtration membrane; third, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 9.0. Subsequently, the two types of water sample were spiked with Cr(VI) standard stock solutions of various concentrations, and the aforementioned method was used for detection and analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: B, N-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method with AC and BPDB employed as the carbon, nitrogen, and boron sources. The fluorescence performance of the B, N-CDs was excellent and could be visualized under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The QY of B, N-CDs is 59.01, while that of undoped CDs is only 14.97%. The QY of quinine sulfate is 54% and the refractive index of the sample and reference is the same, which is true for measurements in the same solvent system. The relationship between the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance of quinine sulfate and B, N-CDs was shown in Fig. S1. The QY of B, N-CD was calculated as 59.01% according to formula (1). The same steps were repeated to obtain the QY of the undoped CDs.

Fig. 1A depicts the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of the B, N-CDs. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the B, N-CDs has a clear absorption peak at 302 nm, which was caused by the n-π* transition of the CO bond; the peak is a typical characteristic of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of fluorescent CDs [19,20]. Moreover, the illustration in Fig. 1A shows that the aqueous B, N-CD solution was light yellow and transparent in sunlight but exhibited bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet radiation at 365 nm. Furthermore, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the B, N-CDs, determined through three-dimensional scanning with the fluorescence spectrophotometer, were 335 and 414 nm, respectively. Fig. 1B shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the B, N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs increased initially and then reached a maximum with an increase in the excitation wavelength from 300 to 340 nm; the intensity then decreased gradually, showing the entire range of excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission behavior. With an increase in the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence peak clearly red-shifted. This excitation-wavelength-dependent behavior could have been due to different particle sizes or diverse surface emissive trap sites [21].

The morphology of the B, N-CDs was characterized using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 2A. The prepared B, N-CDs can be seen to have been monodispersing particles with favorable dispersion, no agglomeration, and near-spherical shape. Because of the (020) diffraction surface of graphitic carbon, the B, N-CDs had a clear lattice structure [22]. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the particle size of the B, N-CDs was generally in the range of 1.2–7.4 nm, and the average particle size was approximately 3.3 nm (23.79%).

For insight into the elemental composition and valence states of the B, N-CDs, XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy were performed. Fig. 3A shows that the XPS survey spectrum of the B, N-CDs contained four dominant peaks at 285.42, 532.37, 401.87, and 192.11 eV corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, and B1s, respectively [23]. The content of the four elements was calculated as 48.51%, 41.84%, 5.99%, and 3.66%, respectively. The C1s (285.42 eV) spectrum (Fig. 3B) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.12 eV (C=C), 285.88 eV (CN), 286.68 eV(C–OH) and 288.48 eV (C=O) [22]. Thus, numerous oxygen-containing groups existed on the surface of the B, N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3C, the XPS spectrum has three deconvolution N1s peaks at 398.98, 399.68, and 400.78 eV attributed to pyridinic, NH as well as pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively [24]. The O1s (532.37 eV) spectrum (Fig. 3D) of the B, N-CDs was deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.48 and 533.08 eV, which were assigned to CO and C–O–C/C–OH groups, respectively [20]. Regarding the B1s spectrum (Fig. 3E), the two fitted peaks at 192.58 and 193.68 eV were ascribed to BO and BC, respectively [25]. All these data revealed that N- and B-containing groups existed in the B, N-CDs.

Fig. 3F presents the FT-IR spectrum, which reflects the approximate structure and composition of the B, N-CDs. The peaks at 3375.78, 3231.63, and 2987.19 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of OH, NH, and CH groups, respectively [20]. The surface of the B, N-CDs contained numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, which have favorable hydrophilicity. The symmetrical carbonyl stretching at 1712.89 cm−1, as well as the stretching of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, concluded that carboxylic acid groups were successfully synthesized on the surface of nanoparticles [26]. The peak at 1601.96 cm−1 was associated with the CC stretching vibration [23]. The existence of this peak indicated the formation of unsaturated CC bonds in the carbon cores, which was consistent with the lattice spacing result. Moreover, the FT-IR peaks at 1394.06 and 1102.67 cm−1 were attributed to BO and CB stretching, respectively [27]. The FT-IR results confirmed that N- and B-containing functional groups were formed through hydrothermal synthesis and successfully doped into the skeleton of the CDs, consistent with the XPS results.

The quenching and recovery of the fluorescence of CDs can be used for detecting analytes. Recently, many studies have investigated the fluorescence quenching of CDs through various quenching mechanisms, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and the IFE [28,29]. Static quenching occurs when a nonfluorescent ground state complex is formed through the interaction of CDs with a quencher [28,30]. By contrast, dynamic quenching is usually caused by collisions between fluorophores and quenchers [17,28]. In the PET mechanism, electron transfer occurs between CDs and the quenching agent, creating cation and anion free radicals, respectively. In this process, a complex is formed between the electron donor and electron acceptor, which can return to the ground state without emitting fluorescence [28,31]. FRET is a nonradiative transition process in which energy is transferred to the energy receptor through long-distance dipole–dipole interaction when the donor is in the excited state. Generally, the emission spectrum of the energy donor and the absorption spectrum of the energy receptor must overlap, and the distance between them must be within 10–100 Å [32,33]. The IFE refers to the absorption of an absorber upon the excitation or emission of light within the detection system when the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores overlap with the absorption spectra of the absorber [32,34]. The IFE is sometimes called apparent quenching because it is not a quenching process but rather the consequence of attenuation of the excitation beam or the absorption of emitted radiation by an excess concentration of CDs or by the quencher in the solution [28].

To elucidate the mechanism through which the B, N-CDs exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for Cr(VI) detection, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the B, N-CDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) were measured. As shown in Fig. 4A, the UV–Vis absorption peak of Cr(VI) is clear at 372 nm, and the excitation and emission peaks of the B, N-CDs are at 335 and 414 nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) thus overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of the B, N-CDs, which leads to Cr(VI) absorbing and shielding some of the emission and excitation light of the B, N-CDs. Moreover, the spectra indicate that the fluorescence quenching of the B, N-CDs is caused by the IFE or FRET [35]. Fig. 4B presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the B, N-CDs, Cr(VI), and the mixture of B, N-CDs with Cr(VI) as well as the spectrum formed through algebraic addition of the absorbance of the B, N-CDs and Cr(VI) at the same wavelength. The addition spectrum and spectrum of the B, N-CD plus Cr(VI) mixture did not overlap within the experimental error range, indicating that B, N-CD–Cr(VI) complexes were formed in the ground state. This result predicted the static quenching mechanism of the entire system [36,37]. To further prove the quenching mechanism of the B, N-CDs, the lifetime of the B, N-CDs without and with Cr(VI) was measured at 337 nm. The results are displayed in Fig. 4C. By fitting a third-order exponential function to the fluorescence lifetime map, the fluorescence lifetime of the B, N-CDs in the absence or presence of Cr(VI) was calculated to be 10.96 and 11.08 ns, respectively. The difference in fluorescence lifetime could be ignored, and the findings confirmed that the entire process was one of static quenching [38]. In addition, the effect of temperature on fluorescence quenching was determined when the detection conditions were optimized. The results revealed that fluorescence quenching was independent of temperature [39]. All the presented findings indicated that the fluorescence quenching of the B, N-CDs by Cr(VI) was due to the static quenching and the IFE.

Their high quantum yield makes it possible for B, N-CDs to be used as fluorescent probes. The experimental results demonstrated that Cr(VI) effectively quenched the fluorescence of the B, N-CDs, indicating that the B, N-CDs can be used as fluorescence probes for Cr(VI) detection.

To obtain the best response signal, the following parameters were optimized: (a) concentration of B, N-CDs (0.01–0.10 mg/L); (b) pH (5–11); and (c) reaction time (0–10 min). Fig. 5A shows that when the B, N-CD concentration was increased, the system's fluorescence quenching rate F/F 0 [where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively] remained stable. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching rate was not directly related to the concentration of B, N-CDs. Moreover, Fig. 5B shows that for a given Cr(VI) concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the entire system was its maximum when the pH of the buffer solution was 9, and this value was used in the subsequent research. Fig. 5C shows that the fluorescence of the B, N-CDs was quenched by Cr(VI) within 6 min. In the subsequent experiments, 8 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for obtaining stable and accurate experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of B, N-CDs for Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of using the B, N-CDs as a fluorescent probe to detect Cr(VI) was further assessed. Under the optimized conditions discussed in Section 3.4, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at different Cr(VI) concentrations was investigated. Fig. 6A shows that the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs decreased with an increase in Cr(VI) concentration, which indicated that Cr(VI) effectively quenched the fluorescence of the B, N-CDs. As presented in the inset of Fig. 6B, a strong correlation was discovered in the Cr(VI) concentration range of 0.3 to 500 μM, and the correlation coefficient (R 2) was up 0.9986, higher than that of the undoped CDs. Furthermore, the Cr(VI) detection limit was calculated to be 0.24 μM on the basis of the three times the standard deviation rule, and this detection limit was lower than the maximum Cr(VI) concentration in drinking water allowed by the World Health Organization [2]. As shown in Table 1 , compared with the probes reported in the literature, the B, N-CD fluorescent probe proposed in this study has the advantages of a low detection limit, wide detection range, simple synthesis, and low cost. Our findings revealed that the B, N-CD fluorescence system quickly, accurately, and quantitatively detects Cr(VI) in the range of 0.3–500 μM.

In addition to the requirement of selectivity, high specificity is one of the most crucial factors in real sample detection. To evaluate the specificity of the B, N-CDs, Al3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+were selected to be metal ions that could interfere with the Cr(VI) target because they can coexist with Cr(VI) in water. Fig. 7 shows that in the presence of the B, N-CDs system, most of the ions had no or a negligible quenching effect, whereas a strong quenching effect was observed after the addition of Cr(VI). When other ions were introduced in the Cr(VI) solution, the presence of the interfering ions had no clear influence on the fluorescence intensity change of the Cr(VI) assay. These results demonstrated that the B, N-CDs had outstanding selectivity for detection of Cr(VI) over that of other common ions.

Because of potential unknown interference sources in real samples, the proposed method and ICP-MS were compared in the detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples to further explore the method's applicability and reliability. Cr(VI) was not detected in the Yudai River water samples or tap water samples. First, the procedure in Section 2.5 was used to pretreat real water samples. Certain concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–200 μM in increments of 10 μM) were added to the water samples, and the fluorescence intensity decreased with an increase of the Cr(VI) concentration by inches. In addition, a strong linear correlation was obtained between the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-CDs and Cr(VI) concentration by plotting the relationship between (F 0 − F)/F 0 and Cr(VI) concentration in the range 0.3–200 μM. The experimental results were verified using ICP-MS, and the results for the B, N-CDs were consistent with those obtained ICP-MS, which is suitable for the determination of Cr(VI) in actual water samples. Fig. 8A shows the relationship between ΔF and Cr(VI) concentration (range 0.3–200 μM) in the Yudai River water sample; the correlation coefficient was R 2 = 0.9968 and equation was (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.00198C + 0.00977 [C is the Cr(VI) concentration], indicating a favorable linear correlation. Fig. 8B shows the plot of ΔF versus Cr(VI) concentration (range 0.3–200 μM) for the tap water samples; the correlation coefficient was R 2 = 0.9981 and equation was (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.00204C + 0.00371, indicating a strong linear correlation.

Furthermore, by comparing the fluorescence spectrum when different pollutants (Al3+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+) was added to the real water sample containing Cr(VI), the interaction between Cr(VI) and other pollutants has also been studied. The result is shown in Fig. S2, most of the changed in spectral are negligible. Therefore, the accuracy of Cr(VI) detection by this method will not be affected by other pollutants.

The results revealed that B, N-CDs can be successfully prepared using the hydrothermal method with AC and BPDB as the carbon, nitrogen, and boron source. Compared with undoped CDs, the B, N-CDs have higher fluorescence quantum yield, element doping, and surface functionalization, which improve their optical properties and provide favorable conditions for sensitive and rapid detection of Cr(VI) through the static quenching mechanism and IFE. After optimization of the detection parameters, the B, N-CDs were discovered to exhibit a strong linear correlation and a low detection limit. The specificity and sensitivity of the B, N-CDs to Cr(VI) in real water samples are excellent, which shows that the B, N-CDs are suitable for the detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water.

Yueying Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xuetao Hu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Wenting Li: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xiaowei Huang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Zhihua Li: Validation, Investigation. Wen Zhang: Formal analysis, Resources. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by all the funds.

Funding: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant numbers 2018YFD0701001); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 31772073, 31671844); Key Research and Development Program of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118807.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple electrochemical sensing for mercury ions in dairy product using optimal Cu2+-based metal-organic frameworks as signal reporting
